article_meta.title: Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell on June 25, 2025, in Washington, DC

transcript.content:
Summarize the following news article and list its key facts. Respond ONLY with a valid JSON object вЂ” no prose, no markdown fences:
{"summary": "string", "key_facts": ["string","string","string","string","string"], "sources_represented": ["string"]}

Summarize this article:

---
HEADLINE: Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell on June 25, 2025, in Washington, DC
SOURCE: CNN (Opinion)

ARTICLE:

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell on June 25, 2025, in Washington, DC. Kent Nishimura/Getty Images Donald Trump Federal agencies Interest rates Investing See all topics Facebook Tweet Email Link Link Copied! Follow Washington вЂ” The gloves are off in the heated clash between Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell and President Donald Trump. As extraordinary as a criminal investigation of a sitting Fed chair is, PowellГўВЂВ™s rebuke of Trump was in some ways even more shocking. For years, Powell has remained mum even as Trump called him every name and used every insult in the book, repeatedly threatening to fire him. PowellГўВЂВ™s playbook has been to remain neutral, saying his one and only focus was on the FedГўВЂВ™s politically independent mission to maintain maximum employment and low inflation. Everything else was a distraction, Powell maintained. Trump forced PowellГўВЂВ™s hand after the Justice Department served him with subpoenas. But in PowellГўВЂВ™s remarkable show of defiance, he didnГўВЂВ™t focus on his own actions but on defending the Fed against the Trump administrationГўВЂВ™s attack on its independence ГўВЂВ” and, more broadly, the US economy. In his stunning two-minute video Sunday night, Powell characterized the Trump administrationГўВЂВ™s potential prosecution as a threat against the livelihoods of the American public to serve TrumpГўВЂВ™s whims. ГЇ"ВїГўВЂВњThe threat of criminal charges is a consequence of the Federal Reserve setting interest rates based on our best assessment of what will serve the public, rather than following the preferences of the president,ГўВЂВќ Powell said. ГўВЂВњThis is about whether the Fed will be able to continue to set interest rates based on evidence and economic conditions ГўВЂВ” or whether instead monetary policy will be directed by political pressure or intimidation.ГўВЂВќ The fact that Powell spoke out forcefully against the Trump administration this week for the first time is all the more notable because Powell has long treasured and promoted the FedГўВЂВ™s independence, which he sees as tantamount to its mission to help American households live better lives. It enables the Fed to do its core mission: making jobs plentiful and keeping prices in check, even when politically imprudent. An emboldened Powell Economists, academics and former Fed officials broadly agree that safeguarding Fed independence is necessary for the soundness of the US economy. It ensures policymakers can make tough decisions on interest rates that are rooted in economic reality and not the whims of whoever controls the White House. For example, a president may want lower rates to grow the economy and send stocks higher, but that policy could hurt Americans by sending prices out of control. Powell has been an ardent defender of the FedГўВЂВ™s ability to set interest rates free of politics throughout TrumpГўВЂВ™s relentless pressure campaign over the past year. For example, during a press conference in July, Powell was asked about TrumpГўВЂВ™s recent attacks on him and the Fed. Powell didnГўВЂВ™t engage ГўВЂВ“ but offered a long defense of the FedГўВЂВ™s independence. ГўВЂВњIГўВЂВ™ll just say that, so I think that having an independent central bank has been an institutional arrangement that has served the public well. And as long as it serves the public well, it should continue and be respected. If it didnГўВЂВ™t serve the public well, then it wouldnГўВЂВ™t be something that we should just automatically defend,ГўВЂВќ Powell said. ГўВЂВњBut what it gives us and other central banks, what it gives you is the ability to make these very challenging decisions that in ways that are focused on the data and the evolving outlook, the balance of risks and all of things we talk about, and not political factors.ГўВЂВќ Trump noted that the Fed is hardly alone ГўВЂВ“ governments of advanced economies across the world have put distance between monetary policymakers and politicians. ГўВЂВњIf you were not to have that, that would be a great temptation, of course, to use interest rates to affect elections, for example,ГўВЂВќ Powell added. ГўВЂВњAnd thatГўВЂВ™s something that we donГўВЂВ™t want to do.ГўВЂВќ An exterior view of the Marriner S. Eccles Federal Reserve building on November 29, 2025, in Washington, DC. Aaron M. Sprecher/AP Previously, the closest Powell had come to criticizing Trump was in November 2024, when he was asked whether he would leave his post early if Trump demanded it. ГўВЂВњNo,ГўВЂВќ Powell said in a press conference. Asked to elaborate, Powell said, ГўВЂВњNot permitted under the law.ГўВЂВќ Why the about face? Because Powell viewed the potential prosecution as an existential threat that could forever politicize the Fed, undermine its credibility and upset global markets and the economy. ThatГўВЂВ™s part of the reason central bankers from around the world rallied to PowellГўВЂВ™s defense Tuesday. In the statement, the worldГўВЂВ™s central bankers stressed that the independence of central banks is a ГўВЂВњcornerstone of price, financial and economic stabilityГўВЂВќ and that protecting that independence with full respect for the law is critical. The statement praised Powell for serving with ГўВЂВњintegrityГўВЂВќ and for his ГўВЂВњunwavering commitment to the public interest.ГўВЂВќ Fed remains independent, for now Among the many problems with TrumpГўВЂВ™s demands for the Fed to lower rates is the perception of political interference when the Fed does take that action. The Fed in the second half of last year lowered interest rates three times in a row, though Trump has asked for more. Trump has said the United States should have rates that are among the lowest in the world and that he should have a say in rate decisions. To avoid the perception of influence, some Fed observers believe the central bank could hold off on rate cuts for longer. In other words, ridding the Fed of its independence could backfire spectacularly on Trump by making further rate cuts less likely and even emboldening Powell to remain on the FedГўВЂВ™s board after his term as chair ends on May 15. He is serving a concurrent term on the board that runs through 2028. ГўВЂВњMy thought is that I really want to turn this job over to whoever replaces me in ГўВЂВ” with the economy in really good shape. ThatГўВЂВ™s what I want to do,ГўВЂВќ Powell said in December after the Fed announced its latest rate cut, in response to a question posed by CNNГўВЂВ™s Matt Egan on what Powell hopes his legacy as chair will be. The Trump administration also has a tough case to prove against Powell. Investors and economists were puzzled by the latest salvo in the Trump-Powell fight, considering PowellГўВЂВ™s term as chair ends in just four months. The federal investigation also isnГўВЂВ™t sitting well with a growing number of Republicans in Congress, such as Sens. Thom Tillis of North Carolina and Lisa Murkowski of Alaska, along with Rep. French Hill of Arkansas. ГўВЂВњPeople that respond publicly to an investigation, outwardly and firmly, like Powell, are usually in the driverГўВЂВ™s seat,ГўВЂВќ Evan Gotlob, a former federal prosecutor, told CNN. Trump allies have accused Powell of mismanaging the central bankГўВЂВ™s renovation, which became more costly in recent years, and of lying under oath when he described some of the upgrades to senators in June 2025. Peter Navarro, a senior counselor to the president, told Fox Business on Monday: ГўВЂВњWe know he made false statements.ГўВЂВќ ГўВЂВњThe only question is whether he was aware and did it with malice,ГўВЂВќ he said. The Fed has been forthcoming about the renovationГўВЂВ™s cost overruns and its collaboration with other agencies, even posting an FAQ on its website with all those details. The Fed last summer also provided a guided tour of the renovation to a handful of reporters. ГўВЂВњLying to Congress is a tough thing to prove,ГўВЂВќ former federal prosecutor Gotlob said. ГўВЂВњPowell is definitely in a better position than the Justice Department.ГўВЂВќ CNNГўВЂВ™s Matt Egan contributed reporting. Donald Trump Federal agencies Interest rates Investing See all topics Facebook Tweet Email Link Link Copied! Follow
---

```json
{
  "summary": "Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell publicly rebuked President Donald Trump after the Justice Department served him with subpoenas as part of a criminal investigation. In a two-minute video statement, Powell defended the Fed's independence and warned that prosecuting him threatens the central bank's ability to set interest rates based on economic conditions rather than political pressure. This marks Powell's first forceful public stand against Trump despite years of presidential attacks. Powell's term as chair ends in four months (May 15), though he could remain on the Fed board until 2028. The investigation reportedly relates to alleged mismanagement of the Fed building renovation and potential false statements to Congress, claims that legal experts and economists view as difficult to prove.",
  "key_facts": [
    "The Justice Department served Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell with subpoenas, initiating a criminal investigation into his conduct",
    "Powell released a video statement defending Fed independence and characterizing the potential prosecution as politically motivated intimidation that threatens the central bank's ability to set rates based on economic conditions",
    "Trump has repeatedly demanded lower interest rates and attacked Powell for years, but Powell had maintained silence until now, viewing this investigation as an existential threat to Fed independence",
    "The investigation allegedly concerns cost overruns on the Federal Reserve building renovation and claims Powell lied to Congress in June 2025, though legal experts say these charges would be difficult to prove",
    "Powell's term as Fed chair ends May 15, 2026, but he has a concurrent board term running through 2028, and the controversy may actually discourage further rate cuts to avoid the perception of political influence"
  ],
  "sources_represented": [
    "CNN (Opinion)"
  ]
}
```

parsed_output.summary: Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell publicly rebuked President Donald Trump after the Justice Department served him with subpoenas as part of a criminal investigation. In a two-minute video statement, Powell defended the Fed's independence and warned that prosecuting him threatens the central bank's ability to set interest rates based on economic conditions rather than political pressure. This marks Powell's first forceful public stand against Trump despite years of presidential attacks. Powell's term as chair ends in four months (May 15), though he could remain on the Fed board until 2028. The investigation reportedly relates to alleged mismanagement of the Fed building renovation and potential false statements to Congress, claims that legal experts and economists view as difficult to prove.
key_facts: The Justice Department served Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell with subpoenas, initiating a criminal investigation into his conduct

Powell released a video statement defending Fed independence and characterizing the potential prosecution as politically motivated intimidation that threatens the central bank's ability to set rates based on economic conditions
Trump has repeatedly demanded lower interest rates and attacked Powell for years, but Powell had maintained silence until now, viewing this investigation as an existential threat to Fed independence

The investigation allegedly concerns cost overruns on the Federal Reserve building renovation and claims Powell lied to Congress in June 2025, though legal experts say these charges would be difficult to prove

Powell's term as Fed chair ends May 15, 2026, but he has a concurrent board term running through 2028, and the controversy may actually discourage further rate cuts to avoid the perception of political influence
